prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Goal
Title: Reduce bedtime anxiety and fall asleep independently
Description: Help Sam manage fear at bedtime using LEGO-based therapeutic play.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Therapeutic Focus
Bedtime Fear and Sleep Avoidance
Category: BEHAVIORAL
Description: Sam becomes anxious at bedtime, needing a parent to stay until he falls asleep.

## Recommendations
- Build a LEGO Calm Castle as a safe-place visualization
- Gradual fading of parent presence
- Brave Tower — add a brick each night he stays in bed

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-01: Built a small LEGO house and said 'this is where brave kids sleep'

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "LEGO-based therapy for anxiety in middle childhood: a pilot RCT" (2023)
   Key findings: Structured LEGO building reduced anxiety scores by 30% over 8 sessions; children reported feeling 'in control' during building
   Therapeutic techniques: Calm Castle building; feelings color-coding with bricks; gradual exposure through play scenarios

2. "Graduated extinction and parental fading for pediatric sleep problems" (2023)
   Key findings: Gradual parental fading reduced sleep-onset latency by 40%; gains maintained at 6 months
   Therapeutic techniques: Parental fading; bedtime pass technique; positive reinforcement of brave behavior

## LEGO Therapeutic Play
This session should integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- Include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- Include breath-timing cues for breathing exercises: "Breathe in... two... three... four..."
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- Target duration: 10 minutes (approximately 1200 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm so glad you're here with me tonight... I heard you've been having some big feelings at bedtime... and that's okay... Lots of kids feel that way when the lights go out... You know what? I also heard something really cool about you... I heard you built a little LEGO house... and you said it was where brave kids sleep... That was a really awesome idea, Sam... So tonight, we're going to be builders together... We're going to build something special to help those bedtime worries feel smaller... If you have some LEGO bricks near you, you can grab a few now... Any colors or shapes you like... And if you don't have any, that's totally fine... We can imagine building together... Are you ready? Let's begin...

First, let's get really comfy... You can lie down in your bed or sit up cozy with a pillow... Just find a spot that feels good... Now, let's take a big, slow breath together... Breathe in through your nose... like you're smelling a warm cookie... one... two... three... and out through your mouth... like you're blowing on soup to cool it down... one... two... three... Good job, Sam...

Sometimes, when it's time for sleep, our minds get really busy... It's like a box of LEGO bricks got tipped over... and all the thoughts are jumbled up... Some thoughts might feel scary... like dark colored bricks... Some thoughts might feel wiggly... like you just can't get settled... That's what bedtime anxiety can feel like... But here's the secret... You are the builder... You get to decide what to build with those thoughts...

So let's start building our very own Calm Castle... This castle is a special, safe place in your mind... You can go there anytime you feel worried... If you have your bricks, pick up one now... Choose a color that makes you feel safe and calm... Maybe it's a soft blue like a quiet sky... or a gentle green like grass... Got your brick? Good... That's the first brick of your Calm Castle's foundation... Place it down... or just imagine placing it...

Now, let's add a brick for your bravery... Think of one brave thing you did today... Maybe you tried a new food... or you said hello to someone... That's a bravery brick... Add it right next to your first one... Great building, Sam...

Sometimes, worries try to knock on the castle door... So we need to build a strong, friendly guard for our castle... This guard isn't scary... It's kind and strong... It says, "Thank you for the worry, but it's safe here now."... Let's build that guard... Pick two bricks... maybe a bright yellow and a strong red... Snap them together... There's your brave guard...

Now, inside your Calm Castle, it's always peaceful... What's inside? Let's build it... Add a brick for a soft blanket... Add a brick for a favorite stuffed animal... Add a brick for a tiny night-light that glows warm, not bright... You're doing such a good job building this, Sam...

Let's take another calming breath... Breathe in... two... three... four... and slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Feel your castle getting stronger with each breath...

Now, here's a plan for your real bedtime... It's called the Brave Tower plan... Every night you stay in your own bed, you get to add a brick to your very own Brave Tower... You can build it on your shelf or your nightstand... Each brick is proof of how brave you are... And guess what? Mom or Dad can help with this plan... They might sit with you for a little bit at first... then, each night, they'll sit for just a tiny bit less time... This is called "fading"... like a sunset slowly fading away... It helps you practice being the brave builder of your own sleep...

So tonight, after we finish talking, you can try it... Maybe Mom or Dad will sit right next to your bed for five minutes... Then they might move to the chair... Then to the doorway... Each step is you adding another brick to your Brave Tower... You already built the first brick, Sam... when you built that LEGO house for brave kids... You've got this.

Let's practice right now... Close your eyes if you want to... Picture your beautiful Calm Castle... See its strong walls... See its friendly guard... It's your safe place... Now, imagine it's bedtime... You're in your real bed... You feel a little worry... So you visit your Calm Castle in your mind... You tell the worry, "Not right now, it's safe sleep time."... You take a deep breath... and you add an imaginary brick to your Brave Tower... Good... You're practicing...

If you ever feel really stuck, you can always call for a parent... That's okay... They love you so much... But each time you try to settle on your own first, you are being so strong...

Let's build one more thing together... Let's build a tiny LEGO "bedtime pass"... This is a special brick... You can give it to your mom or dad one time each night if you really need them... It's like a superpower ticket... But if you don't use it, you get to add it to your Brave Tower in the morning as an extra-special brick... It's a way for you to be in charge...

Okay, Sam... Let's start to finish up... You have your Calm Castle in your mind... You have your Brave Tower plan... And you know that lots of kids are learning this just like you...

Tonight, after your grown-up says goodnight, remember you are the builder... You can visit your Calm Castle... You can feel proud for each minute you are brave... And every single brick in your Brave Tower will show how amazing you did...

You are a wonderful, creative, and brave builder, Sam... Thank you for building with me tonight... Sleep well, and know you are safe...

Remember, Sam, your mom, dad, or another grown-up who takes care of you is always there to help with this plan... You can show them what we built together... Sweet dreams, builder
